allergens: gluten (possible cross-contamination from noodles depending on type)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of Thai seafood tom yum noodle soup is approximately 400 g, comprising noodles, mixed seafood, broth, and aromatics as typically served at a hawker stall or Thai restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Thai Seafood Tom Yum Noodle (Soup) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Thai seafood tom yum noodle (soup), CR ID F05060130. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated at ~0.3 g/100 g based on the low total fat (1.29 g/100 g) and the predominantly lean seafood protein source; this micro-estimate is documented in notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles as analogous food
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai seafood tom yum noodle soup. GI of 53 is estimated from rice noodles (GI ~53, low category) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (53 × 34.0 g carbohydrate in 400 g serving) / 100 = 18.0, rounded to 18.1. The soup broth and seafood protein further moderate the glycaemic response.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 420 mg/100 g; a 400 g serving delivers approximately 1680 mg sodium, representing 73% of the WHO daily limit of 2300 mg.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce and tom yum paste, ask for broth on the side to control intake, and limit portion size to no more than 200 g to keep sodium below 840 mg per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 8.5 g/100 g from moderate-GI rice noodles (GI ~53); a 400 g serving provides approximately 34 g available carbohydrate with a glycaemic load of ~18.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total portion size, avoid adding sweet chilli sauce or extra sugar, and pair the dish with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycaemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 420 mg/100 g is the primary cardiovascular concern; total fat is low at 1.29 g/100 g and saturated fat is estimated at ~0.3 g/100 g, which is favourable for lipid profiles.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size to manage sodium load, choose lean seafood varieties, and avoid adding extra fish sauce or salty condiments to keep the dish within a heart-healthy sodium budget.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Seafood provides lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with anti-inflammatory benefit; however, sodium at 420 mg/100 g may worsen RA-related tissue oedema, and chilli (nightshade) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request reduced fish sauce to lower sodium, consider omitting chilli if nightshade-sensitive, and benefit from the anti-inflammatory lemongrass, galangal, and seafood omega-3 content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Thai Seafood Tom Yum Noodle Soup (ก๋วยเตี๋ยวต้มยำทะเล) is a Thai-style noodle dish consisting of rice noodles simmered in a spicy and tangy tom yum broth with mixed seafood such as prawns, squid, and fish balls. The broth is flavoured with lemongrass, galangal, kaffir lime leaves, fish sauce, chilli, and lime juice, producing a characteristic hot-sour-savoury taste profile. The dish is commonly served as a one-bowl meal at Thai restaurants and hawker stalls across Singapore and Southeast Asia. Nutritionally, it is relatively low in energy at 58 kcal/100 g, with moderate protein (3.2 g/100 g) from seafood, low fat (1.29 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (8.5 g/100 g) from rice noodles. The primary clinical concern is sodium at 420 mg/100 g, driven by fish sauce and tom yum paste; a standard 400 g bowl delivers approximately 1680 mg sodium. The glycaemic index is estimated at 53 (low category) based on rice noodles, making it suitable for moderate carbohydrate management when portion-controlled. Lemongrass and galangal contribute polyphenolic anti-inflammatory compounds, and seafood provides some omega-3 fatty acids. The dish is dairy-free and generally halal-suitable, but contains crustacean, fish, and mollusc allergens.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (CR ID F05060130) at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source. The food identity is exact. Saturated fat is estimated from total fat composition and is documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using rice noodles as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, which is the standard defensible approach for composite dishes without direct GI measurement.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (0.3 g/100 g) is estimated from total fat (1.29 g/100 g) based on the predominantly lean seafood composition; this value is not directly reported in the HPB source and is flagged as an approximation. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite Thai noodle soup dish are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry F05060130 as of 2026. 3. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodles (University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson et al. 2021); no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 4. The input record incorrectly classified this dish under primary_cuisine: 'western' and ethnic_tags: ['western']; corrected to Thai cuisine based on dish identity. 5. Halal status is assessed as generally suitable given seafood and plant-based ingredients; individual preparation may vary — consumers should verify with the specific vendor.
